_source: Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE) India — SME Cluster Energy Benchmarks
_references: BEE-SME Programme: Ludhiana Forging cluster includes sheet metal stamping units
BEE-GEF-WB Programme: Pune cluster (mixed industries) includes press shop operations
UNIDO Industrial Energy Efficiency — Sheet Metal Forming benchmarks
European Commission BREF document: Smitheries and Foundries (2005) — stamping SEC ranges
_units: kWh per tonne of finished product
_notes: Stamping is predominantly electrical (press motors, hydraulics, air compressors). SEC is low for cold processes but yield loss is high (blanking scrap 20-35%). Hot stamping approaches forging SEC due to heating requirement.

cold_stamping_mild_steel.notes: Cold progressive stamping. Low SEC (press + compressor only) but low yield — 25-30% blank scrap typical. Common in auto component MSMEs (Pune, Faridabad clusters).

cold_stamping_alloy_steel.notes: Higher press tonnage required for alloy grades. Slightly lower yield due to springback and trimming.

hot_stamping_steel.notes: Press hardening / hot stamping for AHSS/boron steel. Heating to 900-950 deg C required. Energy profile similar to forging.

cold_stamping_aluminium.notes: Aluminium sheet stamping — lower press forces than steel. Good for automotive body panels.

cold_stamping_stainless_steel.notes: Higher work hardening rate requires more press energy and tool maintenance. Lower yield due to trimming.

deep_drawing_steel.notes: Deep drawing (cups, cylinders) — multiple pass operation increases SEC. Lower yield due to blankholder waste.

deep_drawing_aluminium.notes: Aluminium deep drawing — beverage cans, auto parts. Relatively low SEC.

emission_factors._source: IPCC AR5 / World Steel Association / International Aluminium Institute

grid_emission_factor._source: CEA CO2 Baseline Database for Indian Power Sector, Version 18 (2023-24)
